title: Getting Started
text: The purpose of the ml_research_trends python package is to gather data, clean, and analyze machine learning topics and how they have changed over time. Research papers are taken from SemanticScholar using their API.

title: Getting Started
text:
Requirements
To use the ml_research_trends package you must have each of the following:

Python >= 3.11
Semantic Scholar API key (stored in a .env file)
python-dotenv package for loading environment variables

title: Getting Started
text:
Installation
pip install ml-research-trends
Or with uv:
uv add ml-research-trends

title: Getting Started
text:
Importing
To import the entire package:

import ml_research_trends

To import individual functions:

from ml_research_trends import (
    collect_topic_data,
    summarize_topic_data,
    analyze_topic_trends,
    plot_topic_counts_by_year,
    embed_and_plot_abstracts,
    plot_landmark_timeline
)

title: Getting Started
text:
Setting up API Key
To load your API key from a .env file, use the python-dotenv package:

from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os

# Load environment variables from .env file
load_dotenv()

# Get the API key
SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY = os.getenv('SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY')

Make sure your .env file contains:
SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY=your_api_key_here

title: Getting Started
text:
Using the ml_research_trends package

Step 1: Gather Data
First you need to create your dataset using the SemanticScholar API. You can do this using the collect_topic_data function.
This queries Semantic Scholar for each keyword, de-duplicates the results, filters by year, and returns a tidy pandas.DataFrame. It also writes the DataFrame to CSV if save_path is given.

df = collect_topic_data(
    keywords=[
        "self-attention neural network",
        "multi-head attention",
        "transformer language model",
        "encoder decoder transformer",
    ],
    max_results_per_keyword=50,
    min_year=2016,
    max_year=2026,
    api_key=SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY
)

print(df[["title", "year", "citation_count", "venue"]].head())
print("Total papers:", len(df))

Fetching papers:   0%|          | 0/4 [00:00<?, ?req/s]Fetching papers:   0%|          | 0/4 [00:00<?, ?req/s, keyword=self-attention neural network]Fetching papers:  25%|██▌       | 1/4 [00:01<00:03,  1.15s/req, keyword=self-attention neural network]Fetching papers:  25%|██▌       | 1/4 [00:01<00:03,  1.15s/req, collected=0, keyword=self-attention neural network]Fetching papers:  25%|██▌       | 1/4 [00:01<00:03,  1.15s/req, keyword=multi-head attention]                                                                                                                   Fetching papers:  25%|██▌       | 1/4 [00:01<00:03,  1.15s/req, keyword=multi-head attention]


Rate limited (429). Waiting 1s before retry...


Fetching papers:  50%|█████     | 2/4 [00:03<00:03,  1.77s/req, keyword=multi-head attention]Fetching papers:  50%|█████     | 2/4 [00:03<00:03,  1.77s/req, collected=50, keyword=multi-head attention]Fetching papers:  50%|█████     | 2/4 [00:03<00:03,  1.77s/req, keyword=transformer language model]        Fetching papers:  75%|███████▌  | 3/4 [00:04<00:01,  1.52s/req, keyword=transformer language model]Fetching papers:  75%|███████▌  | 3/4 [00:04<00:01,  1.52s/req, collected=100, keyword=transformer language model]Fetching papers:  75%|███████▌  | 3/4 [00:04<00:01,  1.52s/req, keyword=encoder decoder transformer]              Fetching papers: 100%|██████████| 4/4 [00:05<00:00,  1.33s/req, keyword=encoder decoder transformer]Fetching papers: 100%|██████████| 4/4 [00:05<00:00,  1.33s/req, collected=150, keyword=encoder decoder transformer]Fetching papers: 100%|██████████| 4/4 [00:05<00:00,  1.40s/req, collected=150, keyword=encoder decoder transformer]

1  Three-dimensional DenseNet self-attention neur...  2022              81

Total papers: 200







Step 2: Summarize and analyze

summary = summarize_topic_data(df)
print("Total papers:", summary["total_papers"])
print("Year range:", summary["year_min"], "-", summary["year_max"])
print("Median citations:", summary["median_citations"])

trends = analyze_topic_trends(df)
print(trends)

Total papers: 200
Year range: 2019 - 2026
Median citations: 43.0
   year  paper_count  average_citations  median_citations
0  2019           12         251.166667             159.5
1  2020           22         724.500000             134.5
2  2021           27        1564.037037              65.0
3  2022           36         312.222222              52.0
4  2023           47          74.425532              38.0
5  2024           45          51.000000              24.0
6  2025           10          15.300000              13.5
7  2026            1         166.000000             166.0




Step 3: Make plots

Year counts

plot_topic_counts_by_year(
    trends, 
    topic_name="Transformer Architecture"
)
None










Landmark Timeline

plot_landmark_timeline(
    df,
    landmark_date="2017-06-12",
    landmark_label="Vaswani et al. — Attention Is All You Need",
    topic_name="Transformer Architecture",
)
None










Embedding Abstracts
Note: Embedding the first time will download the model (~8 GB) and cache the vectors to cache_path. After that, rerunning is fast.

embed_and_plot_abstracts(
    df,
    model_name_or_path="Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
    reducer="umap",
    cache_path="cache/tutorial_embeddings.npy",
    topic_name="Transformer Architecture"
)
None

Warning: You are sending unauthenticated requests to the HF Hub. Please set a HF_TOKEN to enable higher rate limits and faster downloads.

title: Home
text:
A project that looks at how a few different areas of machine learning research have grown over time. We pulled papers from the Semantic Scholar API, embedded their abstracts with Qwen3-Embedding-4B, and made a small Python package + Streamlit app to explore the results.



Tutorial — how to install the package and use it end-to-end
Documentation — reference for each function in the package
Technical Report — motivating question, methodology, and findings
Streamlit App — interactive dashboard
GitHub Repository — source code and data





Transformer Architecture
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
Reasoning / Chain-of-Thought
Multimodal / Vision-Language Models

title: Home
text:

title: Documentation
text: Reference for every function exposed by the ml_research_trends package.

title: Documentation
text:
collect_topic_data
collect_topic_data(
    keywords,
    max_results_per_keyword=100,
    min_year=None,
    max_year=None,
    save_path=None,
    api_key=None,
    request_delay=1.1,
    max_retries=5,
    fields=None,
) -> pandas.DataFrame
Query the Semantic Scholar Graph API for each keyword, de-duplicate the results, filter them by year, and return one combined DataFrame.
Arguments

keywords (list[str]): search strings to send to the API.
max_results_per_keyword (int): cap on papers collected per keyword.
min_year, max_year (int or None): inclusive year filter.
save_path (str or None): if given, the DataFrame is written to this CSV.
api_key (str or None): Semantic Scholar API key. If not given, it’s read from the API_KEY environment variable (a .env file is supported).
request_delay (float): seconds to wait between requests (rate limit).
max_retries (int): number of retries on HTTP 429.
fields (str or None): comma-separated list of API fields to request.

Returns
A pandas.DataFrame with columns: paper_id, title, authors, year, citation_count, abstract, url, venue, keyword.
Example

df = collect_topic_data(["chain of thought"], max_results_per_keyword=100, 
api_key=SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY)

title: Documentation
text:
summarize_topic_data
summarize_topic_data(df) -> dict
Return a dictionary of quick stats about a topic DataFrame: total_papers, year_min, year_max, average_citations, median_citations, missing_abstracts, and top_venues.
Example

summary = summarize_topic_data(df)
print(summary["total_papers"], summary["year_min"], summary["year_max"])

100 2022 2025

title: Documentation
text:
analyze_topic_trends
analyze_topic_trends(df) -> pandas.DataFrame
Group papers by year and compute paper_count, average_citations, and median_citations. Useful input for plot_topic_counts_by_year.
Example

trends = analyze_topic_trends(df)

title: Documentation
text:
plot_topic_counts_by_year
plot_topic_counts_by_year(
    trend_df,
    topic_name="Topic",
    figsize=(10, 6),
    show=True,
    save_path=None,
) -> matplotlib.figure.Figure
Bar chart of papers per year. Expects the DataFrame returned by analyze_topic_trends.
Example

plot_topic_counts_by_year(trends, topic_name="RAG", save_path="rag.png")
None

title: Documentation
text:
embed_and_plot_abstracts
embed_and_plot_abstracts(
    df,
    model_name_or_path="google/embeddinggemma-300m",
    device=None,
    reducer="umap",          # or "tsne" / "pca"
    batch_size=8,
    max_papers=None,
    cache_path=None,
    topic_name="Topic",
    random_state=42,
    normalize_embeddings=True,
    trust_remote_code=False,
    umap_kwargs=None,
    tsne_kwargs=None,
    pca_kwargs=None,
    figsize=(11, 7),
    show=True,
    save_path=None,
    html_path=None,
) -> pandas.DataFrame
Embed each paper’s abstract with a Sentence-Transformers model, reduce to 2-D (UMAP / t-SNE / PCA), and produce a scatter plot colored by year. Embeddings are cached to cache_path so subsequent runs don’t re-encode.
Returns
A DataFrame with columns paper_id, title, year, x, y.
Example

coords = embed_and_plot_abstracts(
    df,
    model_name_or_path="Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
    reducer="umap",
    cache_path="cache/docs_embeddings.npy",
)
None

Loading Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B on cpu...

title: Documentation
text:
plot_landmark_timeline
plot_landmark_timeline(
    df,
    landmark_date,
    landmark_label="Landmark paper",
    cumulative=False,
    topic_name="Topic",
    figsize=(11, 6),
    show=True,
    save_path=None,
) -> pandas.DataFrame
Line plot of papers per year with a vertical dashed line marking a landmark paper’s date. Also prints how many papers came before vs. after the landmark.
Example

plot_landmark_timeline(
    df,
    landmark_date="2020-05-22",
    landmark_label="Lewis et al. — RAG",
    topic_name="RAG",
)
None

title: Report
text:
How has the volume of ML research on a handful of high-impact sub-topics grown over time, and how much of that growth can be tied to specific landmark papers?
The intuition is that a single influential paper (e.g. Attention Is All You Need) can kick off a wave of follow-up work. We wanted to see whether that pattern actually shows up in the data for several well-known sub-fields, and also whether the papers in each sub-field cluster together semantically in a way that reflects the topic’s sub-structure. We also wanted to see if there was a delay in how soon the research followed these landmark studies.



We collected paper metadata from the Semantic Scholar Graph API using keyword search. Four topics were included:








Topic
Keywords
Landmark paper




Transformer Architecture
“self-attention neural network”, “multi-head attention”, …
Vaswani et al. (2017)


Retrieval-Augmented Generation
“retrieval augmented generation”, “RAG”, …
Lewis et al. (2020)


Reasoning / Chain-of-Thought
“chain of thought”, “step by step reasoning LLM”, …
Wei et al. (2022)


Multimodal / Vision-Language
“vision language model”, “CLIP”, …
Radford et al. (2021)



For each topic we pulled up to ~500 papers per keyword, de-duplicated by paper ID, and filtered to the year window. The cleaned CSVs live in local_data/<topic>/.




Collection. collect_topic_data calls the Semantic Scholar search endpoint for each keyword, handling pagination, rate limits, and de-duplication.
Cleaning. Missing abstracts and titles are stripped, year and citation_count are changed to integers, and rows without a year are dropped.
Trend analysis. analyze_topic_trends groups by year and computes paper_count, average_citations, and median_citations.
Landmark timeline. plot_landmark_timeline draws the yearly counts with a vertical line on the landmark paper’s date, and prints the number of papers before vs. after it.
Semantic embeddings. embed_and_plot_abstracts encodes each abstract with Qwen3-Embedding-4B and reduces the resulting 4096-dim vectors to 2-D with UMAP (cosine metric, n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.1). Embeddings are cached to .npy so the whole pipeline is re-runnable in a few seconds.
Dashboard. The Streamlit app (app.py + pages/) loads the cached CSVs and embeddings, re-runs UMAP on demand with user-chosen hyperparameters, and renders an interactive Plotly scatter plot.





The landmark-paper effect is real and visible. For every topic, the per-year paper count is basically flat before the landmark paper and then grows sharply afterward. Transformer papers, for example, are dominated by post-2017 work; RAG and CoT both show near-vertical growth immediately after 2020 / 2022 respectively.
Growth is still accelerating. Some of the topics have their highest paper counts in the most recent full year in the dataset. Even “older” areas like the original Transformer line are still growing, not plateauing.
Abstract embeddings recover meaningful sub-clusters. In the UMAP projection, papers group into visibly separate sub-communities (e.g. retrieval systems vs. reasoning vs. evaluation benchmarks within the RAG topic). Color-by-year also shows a clear temporal drift across the embedding space, suggesting the language used to describe each sub-field has shifted noticeably over the last few years.
Citations are extremely skewed. In every topic the median citation count is small but the mean is much larger, driven by a handful of very high-citation landmark papers. This is why the Streamlit app reports the median rather than the mean.




Anyone can reproduce every artifact in the repo by:
git clone https://github.com/dallinmj/ml_research_trends.git
cd ml_research_trends
pip install -e .
cd local_data
python run.py           # edit the TOPIC / SLUG / KEYWORDS block to pick a topic
The raw CSVs, cached embeddings, and plots are all committed under local_data/<topic>/ so you don’t have to re-run the pipeline to explore the results — the Streamlit app and notebooks will work out of the box.




Semantic Scholar’s search endpoint is keyword-based, so topic coverage is only as good as the keyword list. A paper that uses different terminology will be missed.
The 2026 counts are partial (the year isn’t over yet), so the last bar on every plot is an undercount.
Citation counts are a snapshot at collection time and will drift upward.





Add more topics (diffusion models, RLHF, interpretability, etc.).
Cluster the UMAP space and auto-label the clusters with top keywords.
Compare growth curves across topics on one shared plot.
